planting.note: Divide rhizomes with at least one growing point or plant container-grown specimens in consistently moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, fertile soil in partial shade after frost; hanging lobster claw requires rich, consistently moist soil and warm temperatures to flower — dry conditions, poor soil, or temperatures below 55°F cause the plant to go dormant and halt flowering.

directions: Divide rhizomes or plant container specimens in consistently moist, humus-rich, fertile soil in partial shade; hanging lobster claw is the national flower of Bolivia and one of the most spectacular and instantly recognizable tropical flowers in the world — the inflorescence consists of a long, pendant, drooping flower stalk from which 8–20 red, orange, and yellow bracts hang vertically downward in a precisely alternating, pendant chain, each bract edged in vivid yellow and tipped in green, creating a brilliantly colored, dangling cascade that hangs like a living chandelier from beneath the enormous paddle-shaped leaves. The pendant, downward-hanging display (unusual among heliconias, most of which hold their bracts erect) makes it particularly spectacular when displayed at eye level or above, where the full cascade can be appreciated. Hummingbirds feed from the small, tubular flowers nestled within each bract. It grows best in partial shade with rich, moist soil. The cut inflorescences are among the most prized of all tropical cut flowers and last for weeks in a vase. An essential ornamental in zone 10–12 tropical gardens.

planting.note: Divide rhizomes in spring or plant container-grown specimens in consistently moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, fertile soil in partial shade after frost; wild plantain is a large, vigorous grower that spreads by rhizomes to form impressive clumps — allow adequate space (6–10 feet) for its full lateral spread.

directions: Divide rhizomes or plant container specimens in consistently moist, humus-rich, fertile soil in partial shade; wild plantain is a large Caribbean heliconia that produces some of the most dramatic and brightly colored of all heliconia inflorescences — the erect, upright-held flower spikes consist of stacked, boat-shaped, waxy bracts in vivid combinations of red and yellow, or red, orange, and yellow, alternately arranged in zigzag fashion up the vertical stem, with small, white, tubular flowers visible within the bracts' waxy cavities. The inflorescences are produced year-round in the tropics on mature clumps and are exceptionally long-lasting as cut flowers (3–4 weeks). The large, paddle-shaped, banana-like leaves on the vegetative pseudostems provide bold tropical foliage throughout the year. Wild plantain is larger and more vigorous than most Heliconia psittacorum types and requires more space. It grows best in warm, humid, partially shaded conditions with consistently moist, fertile, organic soil. An important nectar plant for hermit hummingbirds in its native Caribbean range. Excellent as a large-scale tropical landscape specimen or in naturalistic tropical garden settings.

planting.note: Divide offset pups from the base of established clumps or plant container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, fertile soil in partial shade after frost; ornamental banana requires rich, moist, organic soil and grows most rapidly with monthly fertilization during the growing season — poor soil and drought produce stunted, unattractive plants.

directions: Divide offset pups or plant container specimens in moist, humus-rich, fertile soil in partial shade; ornamental banana is an Indian and Southeast Asian tropical banana grown for its ornamental qualities rather than its edible fruit — the small fruit clusters are not edible — and is notable for producing one of the most beautiful inflorescences of any banana, with dramatically ornate, upright flower spikes bearing brilliant, deep-rose-pink to purple-pink, waxy bracts that contrast with the small, pale-yellow to white flowers and create a vivid and decorative floral display that is distinctly more ornamental than the simple, greenish-yellow flower bracts of fruiting bananas. The plant's relatively modest stature (5–10 feet), its tolerance of partial shade, and its cold-hardiness to around 28–30°F in zone 9 (the underground rhizomes surviving and reshooting after the tops are killed by frost) make it a practical ornamental banana for Gulf Coast zone 9–12 gardens where the plant can be used as a permanent specimen. The broad, paddle-shaped, typically unspotted green leaves provide bold tropical texture. Spreads by producing basal offsets (pups).

planting.note: Plant corms 2–4 inches deep in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade in early spring; the tuber will flower without being planted at all — a bare voodoo lily tuber placed in a pot without soil or water will flower using energy stored in the tuber, then must be planted to develop foliage and replenish energy for the following year.

directions: Plant corms in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; voodoo lily (monarch of the East, red calla) is a South Asian aroid that performs one of the most remarkable botanical tricks available to the home gardener — the large, smooth, gray-mottled, flattened tuber, when stored through winter and brought out in spring, will spontaneously produce a single, dramatic, brownish-maroon, speckled, mottled spathe surrounding a dark-purple-brown, club-like spadix, without any soil or water, powered entirely by energy reserves within the tuber. The flower produces an intensely foul, rotting-meat odor for about 24 hours to attract fly pollinators — a single spectacular performance rather than an extended display. After flowering, plant the tuber in shaded, moist soil, where it produces a single, large, deeply divided, elegantly patterned, dark-green leaf on a mottled stem that provides months of bold tropical foliage. The leaf dies down in fall and the tuber is stored dry through winter. A reliable shade garden conversation piece for zone 6–10, hardy to approximately 0°F when mulched.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant tubers 2–4 inches deep in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade after frost; Amorphophallus bulbifer is distinguished from A. konjac by producing small aerial bulbils (tiny tubers) in the axils of its leaf divisions — these bulbils fall and self-propagate, making this species the easiest amorphophallus to multiply in the garden.

directions: Plant tubers in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; elephant foot yam is an Indian tropical aroid that produces a brief, dramatic, pink-green and mottled spathe over a dark, club-shaped spadix in spring (with a mild carrion odor for one day), then emerges with one or two enormous, intricately divided, single compound leaves — each leaf rising from a single upright stem on a dramatically patterned, mottled, snake-skin-like, gray-green and brown-spotted petiole, spreading the leaflets of a single deeply compound leaf into an elegant, tropical umbrella form reaching 2–4 feet across. The most distinctive feature distinguishing A. bulbifer from other amorphophallus is the production of small, brown, edible aerial bulbils (tiny tubers, about the size of large marbles) that form in the leaf axils at the junction of the leaflets — these bulbils drop and self-propagate freely in suitable warm, shaded conditions. More cold-tolerant than the similar A. konjac in zone 7. An outstanding, easily grown, and self-multiplying shade garden aroid for tropical and warm temperate gardens.
